text: The robotic pelvic accentuation begins transanally with a full thickness rectotomy made approximately one centimeter proximal to the dentate line, entering the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane from the transanal approach.

The surgeon enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, continuing dissection down to the pelvic floor to connect with the previously established transanal dissection. The dissection progresses circumferentially along the right and left lateral TME planes.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the left ureter, maintaining a cuff of tissue around it to preserve vascular integrity. The dissection extends from the pelvic brim to the vesico-ureteric junction, preparing for the subsequent ileo-ureteric anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the right ureter as it crosses the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, ensuring a cuff of tissue is maintained around it for vascular integrity. The dissection continues down to the vesico-ureteric junction.

The surgeon mobilizes the urinary bladder anteriorly, opening the pre-vesicle space of Retzius. The pubic bone becomes visible anteriorly, and the previously dissected left ureter is seen. The ureter is transected once the bladder is fully mobilized, aiming for the longest possible length near the vesico-ureteric junction for subsequent anastomosis.

The surgeon continues bladder mobilization laterally and inferiorly, reaching the endopelvic fascia. An energy device is used to divide the inferior and superior vesicle vascular pedicles, with visible separation of the vascular structures.
The left vas deferens is visible and divided using a robotic energy device. The endopelvic fascia is also visible, demonstrating its reflection over the levator ani muscle.

The surgeon transects the right ureter near the vesico-ureteric junction, similar to the left side. The right lateral bladder is mobilized, and the endopelvic fascia is visible again. The fascia is incised, allowing for the mobilization of the prostate and identification of the urethra.

The surgeon performs a urethrotomy and divides the urinary catheter. The dorsal venous complex is sutured to achieve hemostasis. The completion of the dissection is confirmed through transanal digital examination.
The surgeon exteriorizes the previously mobilized colon through the anus, with approximately five centimeters of the colon visible outside the body to facilitate a delayed coloanal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a robotic urological enterocysteoplasty, isolating a 30 cm ileal segment approximately 20 cm from the ileocecal valve. An enterotomy is made, and the posterior wall of an ileourethral anastomosis is constructed. The proximal and distal segments of the isolated ileal segment are transected using a stapler, and the ileal segments are detubularised.

The surgeon opens the small bowel segment along its anti-mesenteric border, extending down to the previously formed ileo-urethral anastomosis. The second segment of the small bowel is divided, reaching the iliourethral anastomosis.

The surgeon restores gastrointestinal continuity by performing a side-to-side stapled anastomosis using a robotic stapler, requiring two firings. The second half of the ileal segment is detubularized along its anti-mesenteric border.

The surgeon sutures the medial aspects of the detubularized ileal segments together to form the posterior wall of the neobladder. The corners of these segments are folded down to the level of the ileo-urethral anastomosis, creating a spherical shape for the neobladder. A self-locking barbed suture is used to facilitate the construction, visibly folding the neobladder into its final shape.

The surgeon forms the anterior aspect of the neobladder by suturing the lateral aspects of the isolated ileal segments. The anterior wall of the ileo-urethral anastomosis is also being constructed, with visible sutures bringing the tissue together.

The spherical shape of the neobladder becomes visible as the superior aspect is closed. An enterotomy is made at the corner of the neobladder, followed by an end-to-end ileo-ureteric anastomosis, with a double J stent inserted to protect the anastomosis.
The surgeon locates the previously dissected left ureter and places a second double J stent robotically into the ureter, extending into the pouch of the neobladder.
